title: AutoProxy: AI Message Intelligence Architecture
description: Buildable specification for AutoProxy's AI/ML layer covering model selection, prompt design, RAG with pgvector, classification pipeline, cost modeling, and Elixir process topology.
tags: autoproxy, ai, llm, architecture, elixir, pgvector, rag, classification, cost-modeling
excerpt:
AutoProxy: AI Message Intelligence Architecture

Executive Summary

AutoProxy is a message intelligence layer that intercepts every message in the Amos Comms domain, classifies it, routes it, and optionally generates or suggests responses. It operates in two modes: on-site (single property operations) and corporate/HQ (multi-property oversight). The system is built entirely in Elixir, uses external LLMs via HTTP APIs, and stores all knowledge in Postgres (including pgvector for embeddings).

Routes messages intelligently, filters noise, suggests responses, auto-answers high-confidence factual queries, detects cross-property patterns, triages reports, and generates executive digests. Problem Statement

Hospitality operations suffer from constant "shoulder tapping" -- staff interrupt each other with questions that could be answered from existing knowledge, routed to the right person automatically, or batched until a natural break. The result is fragmented attention, delayed responses, and operational overhead that scales linearly with headcount.

AutoProxy is an intelligent message proxy layer that sits inside Amos's Comms dom

Core Values

1. **Leverage Over Labor** -- Find the lever, not the grind. If the answer is "hire more people," we haven't looked hard enough.
2. **Radical Honesty** -- "You don't need this" is a valid recommendation. Trust is the product; everything else is a feature.
3. **Builder's Mentality** -- Strategy without execution is a hobby. Every engagement produces something tangible.
4. **Right-Sized by Design** -- Simplicity isn't a limitation, it's a design philosophy. Reject the enterprise-lite

title: Soloscaler Brand Story
description: Five-act narrative arc for the Soloscaler brand with elevator, networking, and pitch versions
excerpt:
The Narrative Arc

Act 1: The Broken Promise

Small businesses have been sold a lie: "To grow, you need to hire. To compete, you need a team. To scale, you need an enterprise stack." For a long time it was true. Growth meant headcount, headcount meant overhead, and the economics of scaling didn't work at small business size.

Enterprise software companies built for Fortune 500 problems and offered "small business plans" that were lobotomized versions of the same bloated product. Agencies charged

title: Soloscaler Name Exploration
description: Evaluation of "Soloscaler" brand name against 10+ alternatives with scoring matrix and recommendation to keep the name
excerpt:
Verdict: Keep "Soloscaler"

The name wins on every dimension that matters. It's an invented category word that Keagan can own entirely, and category creators always win in brand strategy.

Why "Soloscaler" Works

- **Category creation**: The word doesn't exist in the market. Owning a new term means owning the category.
- **Self-explanatory**: "Solo" + "scaler" -- anyone can guess the meaning on first hearing.
- **Dual identity**: Describes what Keagan is AND what he helps clients become. Both cr

Overview

SolOS is the Soloscaler Operating System. Its logo is a Sun. The platform consists of five core tools that work together to give soloscalers and small businesses full control over their data, their time, and their operations.

The stack solves one core problem: **time reclamation**. Whether you are a soloscaler fielding client questions or a startup bleeding hours to shoulder-tapping and disorganization, SolOS eliminates the noise and keeps the signal strong.

title: Soloscaler Product Validation Pipeline
description: Four-wave validation pipeline with kill gates, agent mappings, and execution playbook for validating any Soloscaler product idea
tags: soloscaler, validation, pipeline, process, agents, kill-criteria, gtm
excerpt:
Pipeline Philosophy

Designed for a solo operator. Three principles:

1. **Kill fast, build slow.** Front-load steps that produce kill signals. If a product should die, it dies in Week 1, not Week 6.
2. **Parallel by default.** Independent research streams run simultaneously. Sequential gates only where one step's output is a hard dependency.
3. **Stashable deliverables.** Every step produces a standalone markdown document committed to the knowledge base. No work is lost, even for killed product

title: Amos (Stacker): Project README
description: Overview of the Amos hospitality PMS built with Ash Framework on Elixir/Phoenix, including setup instructions, domain architecture, and project structure.
tags: amos, stacker, pms, hospitality, elixir, phoenix, ash-framework, readme
excerpt:
Amos

> Hospitality property management system built with the Ash Framework on Elixir/Phoenix.

Amos is a ground-up rebuild of a prior NestJS system ("Stacker"), preserving the domain model while leveraging Ash's declarative resource system. It serves as the single source of truth for niche hospitality operations -- a single-business PMS, not a multi-tenant SaaS platform.

This repository contains the API backend only. No frontend code lives here.

Tech Stack

- **Elixir 1.18+** / **Phoenix 1.8*
